mission.title: Install and Configure IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service (IKS)
mission.description: IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service (IKS) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment and management of Kubernetes clusters on IBM Cloud. It is ideal for organizations looking to leverage Kubernetes without the overhead of managing the underlying infrastructure.

steps.title: Create an IBM Cloud Kubernetes Cluster
steps.description:
Use the IBM Cloud CLI to create a Kubernetes cluster. Replace `<cluster-name>` and `<region>` with your desired values.

```bash
ibmcloud ks cluster-create --name <cluster-name> --zone <region> --num-workers 3 --machine-type b3c.4x16
```

After the cluster is created, set the context for kubectl:

```bash
ibmcloud ks cluster-config --cluster <cluster-name>
```
```

This command configures your local kubectl to connect to the new cluster.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Setup
steps.description:
Check the cluster information and node status to ensure everything is running correctly.

```bash
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes
```

Ensure all nodes are in 'Ready' status.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration for Autoscaling
steps.description:
Enable the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) for your deployments. First, ensure metrics-server is installed:

```bash
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml
```

Then, create an HPA for your deployment:

```bash
kubectl autoscale deployment <deployment-name> --cpu-percent=50 --min=1 --max=10
```

uninstall.title: Delete the Kubernetes Cluster
uninstall.description:
To remove the Kubernetes cluster and all associated resources, use the following command:

```bash
ibmcloud ks cluster-delete --cluster <cluster-name> --force
```

This command will delete the cluster and all resources associated with it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kubernetes Cluster Version
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your workloads and configurations. You can use the following command to upgrade your cluster:

```bash
ibmcloud ks cluster-update --cluster <cluster-name> --kubernetes-version 1.30
```

After the upgrade, verify the cluster status:

```bash
kubectl get nodes
```

If you encounter issues, you can roll back to the previous version using the same command with the old version number.

troubleshooting.title: Nodes Not Ready
troubleshooting.description:
If any nodes are not in 'Ready' status, check the node conditions:

```bash
kubectl describe node <node-name>
```

Look for any issues in the events section. Common issues include insufficient resources or network problems.

troubleshooting.title: kubectl Not Configured
troubleshooting.description:
If kubectl commands fail, ensure that the context is set correctly:

```bash
ibmcloud ks cluster-config --cluster <cluster-name>
```

Then, verify the context:

```bash
kubectl config current-context
```

troubleshooting.title: HPA Not Scaling
troubleshooting.description:
If the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler is not scaling as expected, ensure that metrics-server is running:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
```

Check the logs of the metrics-server pod for errors:

```bash
kubectl logs <metrics-server-pod-name> -n kube-system
```

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for new node types and improved security features.
Enhanced monitoring capabilities and improved autoscaling features.

resolution.summary: A successful setup of IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service includes a running cluster with all nodes in 'Ready' status, kubectl configured correctly, and post-install configurations such as autoscaling enabled.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the IBM Cloud CLI installed and configured with your account.
